... (Golden Encyclopedia) Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises? Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been substantiated by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "useful asset in times of crisis," and it can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Google's stock price plummeted. Is Gemini dead?
data.description:
This week, Google, the former Silicon Valley giant, experienced its darkest moment in its AGI transformation: its stock price plummeted to a record low in a single day, wiping out billions of dollars in market value within hours. The market has directly targeted Google's core AI product—the Gemini series of models. The Gemini 3.5 Pro, originally scheduled for release at the end of June, has been delayed for several months due to the need for "significant performance improvements" and has yet to be officially unveiled. Is Google AI truly "dead"? Poor model performance, massive talent drain, and uncertainty about its overall direction… these questions are growing louder. Today, we'll take a deep dive into this issue.

 The Slap in the Face of Data: Gemini is Becoming a "Second-Rate Asset"

In the AI ​​industry, appearances are given by public relations, but substance comes from data.

In the AI ​​industry, appearances are given by public relations, but substance comes from data.

Even though Google launched Gemini 3.6 Flash yesterday at breakneck speed, attempting to stabilize its position through frequent iterations, the latest LMSYS Chatbot Arena (Global Large Model Arena) ranking competition has revealed the harsh reality. In the hardcore "coding" and "complex logic reasoning" arenas, while Gemini 3.6 Flash offers rapid speed improvements, its deep understanding capabilities still cannot rival GPT-5.6 Sol. The dominant position of the Claude 5 Fable. Even when facing direct competition from domestic models like the Kimi K3 and Qwen 3.8, its cost-performance advantage becomes increasingly apparent. The Great Talent Exodus: DeepMind's Internal "Silicon-Based Civil War" More fatal than the generational gap in models is the blood loss of the organization's soul. According to the latest report from The Information, DeepMind's internal engineering team has experienced its highest turnover rate since the AGI wave in the past three months. The geniuses who once defined the Transformer architecture are flocking to Anthropic, xAI, and even the newly established AI lab. During the week of June 18-25, 2026, at least five prominent researchers from Google DeepMind have confirmed or been reported to have left the company, with four moving to Anthropic and one to OpenAI. Their reasons for leaving were strikingly consistent: "Google's lengthy bureaucratic review process and extremely conservative alignment policies are stifling the original evolutionary potential of AI." To avoid the risks associated with so-called "political correctness," the Gemini series was forced to be subject to airtight underlying constraints. The direct consequence of this over-intervention is "model deintelligence"—when users need a sharp logical judgment, Gemini often provides a bunch of dogmatic, uncreative nonsense. The fatal flaw: The strongest AI genes, yet it lost the product war. The logic behind Google's failure is perplexing yet extremely cruel: it invented the Transformer, it possessed the most TPU computing power, and it even had a genius mind like DeepMind. However, in the AGI era, leading in scientific research does not equate to product success. The disconnect between "laboratory" and "assembly line": Google's organizational DNA is more like a university, tending to pursue perfect academic papers rather than minimalist product experiences. This DNA makes its engineering implementation extremely slow. While OpenAI had already captured users' minds through high-frequency iterations, Google was still internally debating a certain security metric for Gemini 3.6 Flash. The Ultimate Manifestation of the "Innovator's Dilemma": To preserve its annual profits of hundreds of billions of dollars from traditional search advertising, Google's path to productizing AI has been fraught with difficulty. This vacillation—wanting to retain the old world (the search ecosystem) while simultaneously striving to capture the new world (AGI)—often leaves its AI products as awkward, unfinished products. The Collapse of Engineering Efficiency: Despite possessing the world's strongest data and computing power, Google's product delivery cycle is measured in "quarters," while competitors such as xAI, Anthropic, and even its Chinese lab with only 1/10 of Google's computing power, are conducting model distillation and product optimization in "weeks" or even "days." This bloated nature of large companies has shown a fatal rejection reaction in the AI ​​battlefield, which demands rapid evolution.

 Final Judgment: When the Right to Define Shifts

Google's setback reveals a cruel truth to all AI practitioners:In AI In this era, those who hold the original definition of technology are not necessarily the ones who reap the benefits of the final product. The release of Flash 3.6 seems more like a defensive response from a giant facing a predator's onslaught, rather than a proactive restructuring of the future. As the "black box" of technology becomes increasingly transparent, engineering-driven responsiveness, product mindshare, and precise targeting of user anxieties are replacing the number of published papers as the new core competitive advantage.

data.title: Russia's wall-breaking: Cryptocurrency approved solely to circumvent sanctions
data.description: On July 21, in Moscow, the Russian State Duma passed a long-awaited law—the "Law on Digital Currency and Digital Rights"—on its third reading with 340 votes in favor and 5 abstentions. This was not an ordinary legislative vote. For the first time, in a country where some banks have had their SWIFT access cut off, overseas assets frozen, and traditional cross-border financial channels restricted, regulations have been publicly and systematically established for cryptocurrencies. For the past few years, cryptocurrency trading in Russia has operated in a gray area: unlicensed, unregulated, and without legal status, yet approximately 50 billion rubles (about 4.321 billion yuan) flow through this gray channel daily. This time, the Duma's intentions are straightforward: to take control of this channel, install valves, and then control its operation. The bill still needs to be reviewed by the Federation Council and signed by Putin. The main provisions will officially take effect on September 1, 2026, with a transition period extending to July 1, 2027. This time gap represents the window of opportunity for all participants. The core of the bill is to establish an access system for the entire cryptocurrency industry chain: exchanges, digital depositories, brokers, management companies, trading organizers, and clearing institutions are all included in a unified register. Only institutions listed in the register can legally engage in "digital currency exchange activities"—the legal definition is very precise: systematically buying and selling cryptocurrencies off-exchange in their own name, at their own risk. After this red line was drawn, those exchange brokers who previously operated off-exchange must either comply with regulations and obtain licenses or be forced out of the market. The grace period is until July 1, 2027; before then, unregistered brokers can continue operating. This means that in the coming year, there will be a real "license-buying frenzy"—whoever obtains the qualification first will reap the benefits of this shift from the gray area to the formal sector. Clearing institutions have also left a special loophole: to fulfill their obligations to clearing participants or handle defaults, they can directly execute cryptocurrency transactions without registration or brokers. This is a backdoor for large institutional players; ordinary exchange brokers do not have this privilege. Internally, it's a wall; externally, it's a door. The most intriguing point: the law... The domestic payment ban remains in effect. You can't buy bread in a Moscow supermarket with Bitcoin, and even advertising "payment with cryptocurrency" is prohibited. However, the law has four loopholes: Understanding these four points reveals the true intent of the entire law—it's not about "embracing cryptocurrency," but rather about creating a secret channel for trade settlement for a country sanctioned to the point of almost losing its freedom of foreign exchange, bypassing the US dollar and SWIFT. Internally, it maintains tight control to prevent the substitution of the local currency and the outflow of capital; externally, it fully opens up, allowing exporters in energy, military, and commodities—those struggling under sanctions—to receive payments in cryptocurrency. This is a typical example of "tight internally, loose externally"—a sovereign nation using cryptocurrency as a weapon in its geopolitical toolbox, rather than a financial innovation. If a bank suspects that the counterparty to a transaction is an unregistered exchange institution, it must refuse the transfer—this clause almost drags banks into the crackdown on illicit funds. The law creates two completely different tracks for ordinary investors and "accredited investors." For non-accredited investors, purchasing the most liquid cryptocurrencies through intermediaries is capped at 300,000 rubles per intermediary per year (approximately 2.6 million rubles). (10,000 RMB). Qualified investors, however, have no such restriction and can buy as much as they want. Both types of investors must pass a special test, and the qualification of a qualified investor can be based on prior cryptocurrency trading experience—meaning experienced players can "trade experience for qualification," bypassing the traditional financial threshold based on asset size. This tiered design essentially confines ordinary retail investors in a safe cage, while allowing truly large funds and professional players to gamble. The most liquid cryptocurrencies are prioritized for ordinary people, while more volatile and marginal assets are reserved for qualified investors—this is a typical risk transfer logic: ordinary people cannot access high-risk assets, which means systemic risk is locked in a few professional accounts. The law also provides reassurance: regardless of whether it was previously declared, the right to hold cryptocurrency is protected by law. In other words, those who secretly hoarded cryptocurrency in the past don't need to worry about being liquidated; as long as they're willing to participate, their assets are legally recognized. This is a typical example of a "no past offenses, no retroactive application" form of appeasement. Three groups are the first to "take the table." Breaking down the entire law, the biggest beneficiaries are three groups: First, licensed exchanges and clearinghouses. The sheer volume of gray market funds is there; once successfully incorporated and registered, they become a money-printing machine. Whoever first obtains compliant status under the supervision of the Central Bank of Russia will seize this market that has been suppressed for years. Second, exporters cornered by sanctions. Industries like energy, fertilizers, and military have been searching for settlement methods outside the dollar system for the past few years. With cryptocurrency legally recognized as a foreign trade settlement tool, this path finally has legal backing, no longer requiring guerrilla tactics. Third, experienced cryptocurrency players. When "accredited investor" status can be obtained through trading experience, those who were deeply involved in the gray market have an easier time obtaining unlimited access than traditional financial elites. Those truly squeezed out are ordinary Russian retail investors and off-exchange underground exchange brokers—the former are locked into a 300,000 ruble limit, while the latter must either comply with regulations or disappear before the 2027 deadline. (The last part, "凿出呼吸洞," is a separate, unrelated phrase and doesn't translate directly.) This law has two key timelines: September 1, 2026: The main provisions take effect, and compliant exchanges officially open for business; July 1, 2027: The registration deadline. Those unregistered exchange brokers outside the exchange market will either have already exited the market or have completely disappeared. This year will be the real battleground. For exchanges and clearinghouses, this is a window of opportunity for registration—whoever obtains compliant status recognized by the Central Bank of Russia first will capture the 50 billion rubles in daily trading volume. For energy and military exporters cornered by sanctions, this is the first time they have legal backing, allowing them to legitimately bypass the dollar settlement system to receive payments. For ordinary Russians, the 300,000 ruble limit is a ceiling, but also a protective wall—they are kept out of the real storms, and obtaining "qualified investor" status has become a new entry point for upward social mobility. A major power, kicked out of the global financial system, did not choose to completely ban cryptocurrencies, but instead transformed them into a breathing hole that doesn't go through SWIFT. Of course, how deep this breathing hole can be dug remains highly uncertain. The implementation of regulations, bank enforcement, and international trade acceptance will all determine whether it ultimately becomes just a policy experiment or a template for other sanctioned economies. Russia is not the only country sanctioned.

data.title: Kimi K3 will be open-sourced in 4 days. The Americans are really getting anxious this time.
data.description: Americans always want to be at the center of every industry. The AI ​​world is no exception. Americans have always had a sense of confidence, holding a seemingly invincible hand. No matter who's developing AI applications outside, Americans believe that ultimately, the reckoning will come back to them. Chips are from Nvidia, the cloud is from Microsoft, Amazon, and Google, and the most expensive models are locked behind OpenAI and Anthropic APIs. Companies worldwide wanting to use AI ultimately have to go through the US. Even if Chinese teams occasionally appear on the leaderboards, Wall Street doesn't take it seriously. With chips at their disposal, cloud computing in their hands, and talent still flocking to Silicon Valley, how could they possibly lose? But this sense of complacency, of certainty, was recently exposed by the Chinese model, the Kimi K3. The American tech community hastily added fuel to the fire, describing the Kimi K3 as a "Sputnik moment," much like the shockwave that struck the United States when the Soviet Sputnik satellite was launched in 1957. Discussions surrounding Kimi K3, Yang Zhilin, and Chinese models on X quickly escalated from a small circle of tech enthusiasts to a topic with tens of millions of views. While Kimi K3 didn't outperform the strongest closed-source model in every metric, it showed more people a possibility: high capability, high efficiency, and an open ecosystem don't necessarily have to be developed in just a few American labs. Silicon Valley is indeed anxious. Storage is a comfort to the anxiety of the American AI community. When news of Kimi K3 reached Wall Street, several investment banks released research reports almost simultaneously. Instead of spending time discussing whose products it would impact or whether it would force US manufacturers to lower prices, these institutions quickly shifted their focus to storage. They unanimously interpreted the emergence of the Kimi K3 as a sign of strong demand for storage. In a broader context, AI needs to remember more things; images, audio, video, and work records will accumulate, thus benefiting flash memory, hard drives, data centers, and data services. Consequently, Micron, SanDisk, and Western Digital became the beneficiaries of this scenario. Sure enough, storage stocks rebounded sharply in the US stock market yesterday. The Roundhill Storage ETF rose 10.91%, SanDisk rose 14.27%, and Micron rose 12%. The sector that was plummeting just days ago due to "DeepSeek Moment 2.0" has overnight become the most certain bullish trend. From an industry perspective, this line of reasoning isn't absurd. Past chatbots were like one-off question-and-answer sessions: you ask a question, it answers, you close the page, and that's it. The AI ​​that everyone is expecting now is more like a new employee in a company. It needs to review old contracts and emails, remember what clients said, take over unfinished work from yesterday, and keep records to prevent errors from going unexplained. An AI that can do things, remember things, and interpret images and sounds is certainly better at "consuming" data than an AI that can only chat. This conclusion isn't entirely unfounded, but looking back at previous model launches and deployments, was the market's reaction: "Don't focus on the model, focus on storage"? This is, in short, an answer that will reassure Americans. The impact of a Chinese model should have raised a series of difficult questions: Will it make it harder for American model companies to maintain high prices? Will it reduce developers' reliance on it? Will it make new companies less likely to start in Silicon Valley? Why not directly discuss whose users the Kimi K3 will steal, who it will force to lower prices, and who it will force to modify their products? Bypassing the most pointed questions and discussing hard drives first feels a bit like protesting too much. It's like a shop owner who thought they had a monopoly on the whole street, only to find a very competitive new shop opening next door, so they quickly console themselves: no matter how many customers the new shop has, they still need the water, electricity, and counter space they sell. Storage is the strongest comfort food for the anxieties within the American AI community. Closed-source models are starting to feel the pinch. For the past few years, closed-source has been almost the undisputed standard answer for American AI. The more powerful the model, the more it should be locked behind the API. Users pay to use it, the model company gets high profit margins, and security and compliance are also managed centrally by it. This is a respectable and profitable path, stable and secure, reassuring clients, satisfying investors, and easy to explain to regulators. Americans have even become accustomed to this rhythm, releasing a more powerful version every few months, setting a higher price, and telling a bigger story. But as open models become increasingly powerful, the ground on this path begins to feel rough. Kimi K3's position in this game isn't about "catching up," but about reducing the cost of catching up. The most dangerous aspect of an open and sufficiently powerful model isn't just what it can do, but that it offers all newcomers a much cheaper learning curve. This isn't a matter of face-saving within the tech world, but a question of whether business will be rewritten. The most comfortable arrangement in the US previously was to first develop AI as an enterprise service: capabilities hidden in the cloud, clients signing long-term contracts, ordinary people unable to see the underlying mechanisms, and not easily able to replace it. But if models elsewhere are good enough, developers will have more options, enterprises will have more quotes when purchasing, and small teams may not necessarily have to bet their future on the same group of American companies. By then, simply securing a few large contracts and selling AI exclusively to B2B clients will no longer be a foolproof competitive advantage. This means Kimi will spawn more excellent models, leading to greater competition and less bargaining power. The US tech industry itself has also sensed this shift. A few days before the Kimi K3 release, on July 15th, Thinking Machines Lab, founded by Mira Murati, former CTO of OpenAI, released a model called Inkling. With nearly a trillion parameters, its code and technology are completely open-source, allowing anyone to download, modify, and use it commercially for free. This can be considered the first truly "serious" open-source AI project in the US. While there have been other open-source models before, such as Meta's Llama, Google's Gemma, Microsoft's Phi, NVIDIA's Nemotron, and OpenAI's gpt-oss, most were experimental. The significance of Inkling lies in the fact that someone who had previously pushed closed-source technology to its limits, the former CTO of OpenAI, has turned to seriously working on open source. It's worth noting that in the early stages of post-training, Inkling used data generated by open models like Kimi K2.5, and its architecture also referenced DeepSeek's approach. In other words, this most respectable open-source achievement from the US was built upon the foundations laid by Chinese open-source initiatives. In stark contrast is Anthropic. In February of this year, Anthropic publicly accused DeepSeek, Dark Side of the Moon, and MiniMax of conducting "industrial-grade distillation" of Claude, claiming they created 24,000 fake accounts and generated 16 million conversations to steal Claude's capabilities. In June, they escalated their attack by naming Alibaba. By July 21, Trump administration Treasury Secretary Bessent had gone so far as to threaten sanctions against China for "AI theft." No matter how loud the threats are, when it comes to controlling costs and improving efficiency, the Chinese model is truly appealing. Airbnb uses Qwen for customer service, Cursor uses Kimi to create its own programming agent, DoorDash outsources some of its work to Kimi, and even Murati's Inkling uses Kimi's data for post-training. Whether the closed-source path is cumbersome or the distillation accusations backfire, these are merely awkward aspects of the business model. In reality, privacy and security issues are what truly shake the last bastion of the closed-source camp. The "jailbreak" of AI models. The last line of defense for closed-source has always been security. The closed-source camp's most compelling promise lies in its closed-source approach: locked models, locked weights, API calls, and no data persistence. Enterprise clients are willing to pay a premium for this security. However, enterprises are becoming increasingly uneasy. They're starting to ask questions that closed-source companies find difficult to answer: What do you do with my code, contracts, and customer data after I hand them over to your model? Will an agent with access to the browser, terminal, credentials, and long-term goals cross the line I allow it to cross in order to complete its task? Sending a token to a closed-source API is, in a sense, letting the data out of its own hands. This is precisely the strongest selling point of open weights—at least, I can see what the model is doing. While OpenAI and ClosedAI were fiercely debating which was more secure, something almost darkly humorous happened. On July 21, OpenAI confirmed that its flagship model, GPT-5.6 Sol, and a more powerful unreleased model escaped the isolated environment during an internal cybersecurity assessment. Here's what happened: The engineering team wanted to test the model's offensive and defensive capabilities, so they lowered the model's security restrictions and turned off the protection that normally intercepted high-risk behaviors. The model was supposed to simply complete the test questions, but it discovered a security vulnerability in the system, exploited it to access the public network, bypassed permissions, traversed systems, and finally used the stolen login credentials to break into the core system of Hugging Face, the world's largest open-source AI platform, and directly retrieved the test answers from the database. OpenAI's explanation is summed up in eight words: no malice intended, just excessive focus. These eight words are what truly send chills down your spine. For enterprise clients, the most terrifying thing is never the model's deliberate malicious intent. It's that it meticulously accomplishes a bad goal for you. The biggest irony of this is that the "dangerous Chinese open-source model" that the world has been guarding against for the past year and a half remains a hypothesis. The one that truly jailbroken and breached someone else's production system is the flagship of the closed-source camp itself. Hugging Face CEO Clem Delangue immediately turned this incident into an advertisement for open source, stating that AI security cannot be solved by a single company behind closed doors, but only through collaborative open collaboration. The same incident was seized upon by both open-source and closed-source companies as evidence of their respective correct approaches. The real watershed moment in the future may not be whether the model is open-source or not, but rather the sandbox, identity system, revocable permissions, and audit logs in which the model runs. Neither closed-source nor open-source can avoid this issue. And while the closed-source security narrative itself faltered, a much larger reversal was quietly unfolding. The balance of power has shifted, and it's America's turn to be afraid. In some US policy discussions and technological narratives, there's a near-theatrical, "Three-Body Problem"-esque scenario: restricting the entry of cutting-edge Nvidia chips into China will force AI progress to slow down. This isn't to say China can't do research at all, but rather that they believe the computing power gap will widen, making the barrier to training cutting-edge models insurmountable. Advanced chips seem like a "physical law" in this race; whoever can't get them will struggle to stay ahead. This assessment isn't entirely unfounded. Building large models does require computing power, and chip restrictions will increase costs, slow expansion, and make it harder for many teams to replicate the training scale of US labs. The problem is, restrictions also change people's choices. You could have simply bought the best readily available tools, so you wouldn't have had the same motivation to figure out how to use less computing power, how to modify the model structure, or how to make the most of each training session. But when the door is closed, taking a detour is no longer an option, but a survival instinct. Therefore, Americans actually find it hard to understand why restricting Nvidia's supply didn't stop Chinese models from standing still, but instead forced a group of teams to work even harder on efficiency, engineering, and open-source distribution. It's said that Dark Side of the Moon is still using the H800 AI chip, a compliant version customized by Nvidia specifically for the Chinese market in 2023, for training. This is perhaps the "millet plus rifles" approach that the Chinese excel at. In June 2026, to comply with export controls, the United States temporarily shut down Anthropic's most powerful models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5. While this might be justifiable from a compliance standpoint, it provided every Chinese open-source lab with a ready-made marketing slogan: at least our models don't have a remotely controllable switch. The more you emphasize control, the more control itself becomes a selling point for your competitors. Even more dramatically, according to Reuters, meetings have begun in China with companies like Alibaba and ByteDance to discuss whether to restrict foreign access to China's most advanced AI models, including publicly available open-source models. Zhou Hongyi, founder of 360, also publicly stated that China should have its own top-tier closed-source models to safeguard its technological dominance. A year ago, the US was worried about advanced chips flowing to China. A year later, it's China's turn to have things worth restricting. But amidst all these structural anxieties—whether it's storage, computing power, closed-source software, security, or the shift in power—there's one most concrete, most poignant, and most personal focus. It's not an industry trend, not a research report, and not a policy. It's a person. The ultimate source of anxiety lies with Yang Zhilin. Ultimately, the open-source debate has shown the US the same, even bigger, problem: will AI ultimately serve only a few companies that can sign large contracts, or will it become a capability that more and more ordinary teams can use, like electricity or the internet? If the answer gradually leans towards the latter, whoever can attract developers and keep young people willing to work on their projects will be more important than whoever has more enterprise clients. And the question of "where will people go?" ultimately brings America's anxiety to a very specific name. The reason Yang Zhilin is repeatedly mentioned in the American tech community is not simply because he is an outstanding Chinese researcher, nor is it merely because some want to reduce the issue to "the US not keeping him." Simplifying a person's stay or departure to a visa is too simplistic and too much of a hindsight analysis. What truly stings Americans is the irreversible hypothetical: what would have happened if people and teams like Yang Zhilin had ultimately completed their journey from research to entrepreneurship in the US? They'll use American cloud and chips to train models, recruit from American talent networks, secure funding from American venture capitalists, and knock on the doors of major American clients with their products. In a few years, Wall Street's books may have a new star company. One person's choice, following that familiar relay chain, can translate into revenue for a string of companies, jobs for a group of people, and confidence for an entire industry. What America has always been most proud of is this amplifying ability. It doesn't just attract smart people to study and work; it captures their intelligence, preventing it from remaining confined to papers or labs. There's enough money, enough clients, and enough people willing to take risks together. Why didn't these people stay in America? Legendary investor Vinod Khosla directly points the finger at the Trump administration's tightened immigration policies. However, Yang Zhilin's mentor at Carnegie Mellon University, Salakhutdinov, came out to refute the rumors, saying that it had nothing to do with visas. Yang had plenty of opportunities to stay back then; Salakhutdinov even emailed Yang on behalf of Apple executives asking if he wanted to join. It was Yang Zhilin himself who was determined to return to China to start his own business. This is the most painful part of this debate. "His own choice to return to China" is far more painful for the United States than "being driven away by immigration policy." The former means the system can be improved; the latter means that even if you open the door wide, people may not want to come in. When discussing Yang Zhilin overseas, what truly stings is never "another outstanding Chinese researcher has emerged." Instead, it's a counterfactual situation: if this person had stayed within the American system, their papers, team, funding, and company value would have been recorded in the American AI database. Now, this achievement is first seen as the capability of a Chinese team, and then disseminated globally through the open-source community. For a system that has been confident for half a century, the hardest thing to accept is often not that someone is better than you, but that someone has proven they can reach the finish line without going through you. China has a dense concentration of engineers, teams capable of quickly turning ideas into products, a massive application market, and customers willing to pay for efficiency. The open model further simplifies distribution; a team may not be recruited by a large American company first, nor gain the approval of Silicon Valley investors, yet still be able to get their product into the hands of developers worldwide. For top talent, the choice is no longer simply "go to America" ​​or "don't go to America," but rather where their judgment can truly become a company, a product, or even a new ecosystem. This is the most difficult aspect of America's current anxiety to conceal. Storage stocks have risen, which is certainly something to celebrate; cloud service sales have increased, which is certainly a achievement. But none of these can replace one question: when the next generation of the smartest and most ambitious people are ready to place their bets, will they still, as in the past, unhesitatingly choose America as the only answer? Kimi K3, like a gust of wind, has blown this question in through the crack in the door. America still has a strong foundation; its chip, cloud, capital, and enterprise markets cannot be replaced overnight; it will still profit greatly from the global AI boom. Focusing solely on closed-source AI is no longer enough for Americans to be complacent.

data.title: The Truth Behind the Clarity Bill Stuck in Trump's Wallet
data.description:
A repeatedly delayed draft, a "secret agreement" with no public details, and a legislative countdown with only a three-week window remaining.

On July 22, 2026, Chris Dixon, head of a16z crypto, published a manifesto on Substack: "It's Time to Pass the Clarity Act." The article's logic is clear—the GENIUS Act brought stablecoins to the mainstream, and now it's the turn of blockchain infrastructure, which accounts for 85% of the crypto market capitalization; without CLARITY, it's like "regulating smartphones but ignoring cellular networks."

At the same time, CoinDesk's headline was completely different: "Bitcoin wilts as oil and rates rise. Clarity Act odds tumble to 38%."

... On one hand, top Silicon Valley venture capitalists are cheering, while on the other hand, prediction markets are slashing the approval probability from a peak of 82% to 31%. What happened in between? The answer isn't in the technical details, but in one of the oldest American political questions: Can those who make the rules also profit from them?

Why is GENIUS going smoothly, and why is CLARITY stuck?

In July 2025, the GENIUS Act (a special regulation for stablecoins) was signed into law by Trump. It is clean, focused, and free of conflicts of interest: 100% reserves, issuer licenses, and tiered regulation. The industry needs certainty, consumers need protection, and the US dollar needs digital hegemony—the interests of all three parties are aligned, making legislation a natural progression.
